---
title: Latitudinal gradients in the structure and stability of marine food webs in the Southwest Atlantic
bibliography: SouthMarineFoodWebs.bib
csl: "functional-ecology.csl"
output:
  pdf_document:
    keep_tex: true
    includes:
      in_header: margins.sty
---

Leonardo A. Saravia ^1^^2^, Tomás I. Marina^1^, Iara D. Rodriguez^5^, Manuela Funes^8^, Georgina Cordone^7^, Santiago R. Doyle^3^^4^, David E. Galván^7^, María Agullo Samper^5^, Susanne Kortsch^5^^6^, and Fernando Momo^3^^4^

1. Centro Austral de Investigaciones Científicas (CADIC-CONICET). Houssay 200 (9410), Ushuaia, Argentina.

2. Universidad Nacional de Tierra del Fuego (UNTDF), Yrigoyen 879 (9410), Ushuaia, Argentina

3. Instituto de Ciencias. Universidad Nacional de General Sarmiento. J.M. Gutierrez 1159 (1613), Los Polvorines, Argentina.

4. Departamento de Ciencias Básicas. Universidad Nacional de Luján. Rutas 5 y 7, (6700) Luján. Argentina.

5. Ecosystems and Environment Research Programme, Faculty of Biological and Environmental Sciences, University of Helsinki, 00014 Helsinki, Finland

6. Tvärminne Zoological Station, Faculty of Biological and Environmental Sciences, University of Helsinki, 10900 Hanko, Finland
        
7. Centro para el Estudio de Sistemas Marinos (CONICET-CESIMAR), Bv. Almirante Brown 2915 (9120), Puerto Madryn, Argentina.

8. Instituto de Investigaciones Marinas y Costeras (CONICET-IIMyC), Juan B. Justo 2550 (7600) Mar del Plata, Argentina.

## Abstract

Understanding how ecological network structure varies across broad spatial gradients can reveal general principles of ecosystem organization, while also providing insights into the ways anthropogenic drivers[a] shape ecosystem functioning. We analyzed a latitudinal gradient of seven marine food webs (48°S–78°S) spanning from the Southwestern Atlantic (San Jorge Gulf) to Antarctica (the Weddell Sea). We characterized these food webs using a suite of structural and dynamical metrics: connectance, link density, mean trophic level, SVD complexity, modularity, rank deficiency (as a measure of redundancy), and dynamic stability. To account for variability, we generated stochastic simulations from a metaweb-based model and fitted generalized additive models (GAMs) to assess the effects of latitude, ecosystem area, and human impact.

Our analyses revealed distinct patterns across metrics: a) connectance increased with both latitude and area, consistent with theoretical expectations that larger and closer-to-the-poles systems sustain proportionally more trophic interactions; b) SVD complexity increased significantly with latitude, suggesting greater internal organization towards polar regions, whereas human impact showed no effect; c) modularity declined with latitude, area, and human impact, indicating reduced compartmentalization in larger and more disturbed systems; d) rank deficiency decreased with both area and human impact, pointing to greater redundancy in smaller and less disturbed systems; and e) dynamic stability increased with latitude, supporting the view that high-latitude food webs may become more stable through increased network complexity.

Taken together, these findings reveal that food web structure and stability vary systematically across a latitudinal gradient in the Southwestern Atlantic to Antarctic, but not always in ways anticipated by theoretical expectations. The contrasting responses of connectance, modularity, redundancy, and dynamical stability suggest that distinct ecological drivers shape different aspects of network organization. These differences highlight the need to consider multiple facets of network organization when analysing and predicting the impacts of anthropogenic drivers on food webs.

## Introduction

Understanding how ecological network structure varies across broad spatial gradients can reveal general principles of ecosystem organization, while also providing insight into how anthropogenic drivers shape ecosystem functioning. In marine systems, food webs integrate multiple ecological processes—such as energy transfer, species interactions, and functional organization—that collectively determine ecosystem stability and resilience under environmental change. As a result, trophic interaction networks provide a powerful framework for evaluating ecosystem responses to both natural gradients and human‐induced stressors.
Marine food webs across the Southern Hemisphere are exposed to distinct but often overlapping stressors, including fishing pressure, ocean warming, biological invasions, sea‐ice decline, and glacial retreat, each expected to leave characteristic signatures on food‐web structure and stability. In a comprehensive review spanning the Southwest Atlantic and Antarctic regions, @Marina2024b compiled evidence linking dominant regional stressors to hypothesized changes in food‐web connectance, modularity, stability, energy fluxes, and functional diversity. That synthesis highlighted strong context dependency in food‐web responses, suggesting that large‐scale spatial gradients may mediate how stressors translate into network reorganization.
Building on this conceptual framework, several hypotheses have been formulated to explain how environmental and spatial factors influence the structure and stability of marine trophic networks. Here, we adopt a macroecological approach to explicitly test these hypotheses by analysing a suite of topological and dynamical metrics—Connectance, Link Density, Mean Trophic Level, Modularity, Maximum Eigenvalue (local dynamical stability), SVD Complexity, and Rank Deficiency—in relation to broad environmental gradients, including Latitude, Area, Human Impact, Depth, and the number of trophic species (S). This approach aligns with ongoing efforts to identify general laws governing ecological network organization across spatial scales and environmental contexts [@Welti2015; @Cirtwill2015; @Marczak2007].
Several hypotheses guide our analyses. According to the ecosystem size hypothesis, larger ecosystems are expected to sustain longer food chains due to increased species richness, greater habitat heterogeneity, and enhanced persistence of higher trophic levels [@Post2000; @Post2007]. Empirical support for this pattern has been reported across freshwater and marine systems [@Takimoto2008]. However, food chain length is not determined by ecosystem size alone. Community organization and energetic constraints may impose upper limits on trophic structure, particularly when large‐bodied taxa feed at relatively low trophic positions. In such cases, energy pathways to top predators saturate at lower trophic levels, resulting in shorter mean food chain lengths despite high productivity or large ecosystem size [@Pimm2002; @Post2002]. This mechanism predicts that food webs dominated by large herbivorous or omnivorous consumers will systematically exhibit shorter food chains than webs lacking such taxa [@Jardine2016]. Consistent with this view, studies of marine food webs have found no universal latitudinal pattern in food chain length or maximum trophic level, suggesting that biogeographic gradients interact with community composition and energetic constraints rather than acting as simple drivers [@JakeVanderZanden2007].
Another key dimension concerns human impact. Anthropogenic pressures are hypothesized to erode network organization and drive food webs toward more homogeneous or random structures, potentially reducing complexity and stability [@Mestre2022]. However, empirical evidence from marine systems remains mixed, with some studies reporting increased connectance or altered energy pathways under disturbance, depending on ecosystem type and spatial context [@Mestre2022]. In parallel, recent global syntheses indicate that connectance in marine food webs tends to increase toward higher latitudes, although this pattern is partly confounded by variation in species richness and differences between coastal and oceanic systems [@Albouy2019].
Together, these frameworks highlight how ecosystem size, latitude, depth, and human disturbance may jointly shape the organization, stability, and functional complexity of marine trophic networks. By explicitly testing these hypotheses across a latitudinal gradient in the Southwest Atlantic and adjacent Antarctic systems, our study provides a quantitative assessment of how large‐scale environmental gradients structure marine food webs under contrasting disturbance regimes.



Ecological networks can be analyzed using Singular Value Decomposition (SVD), which provides complementary measures of complexity [@Riva2023a]. The rank of the adjacency matrix reflects the diversity of interaction strategies, while the distribution of singular values can be used to compute an entropy measure. Since random networks maximize this entropy, we instead use $1 -$ SVD entropy[a][b][c][d] to capture the organized heterogeneity that characterizes ecological systems.[e]

Ecological networks are often represented by adjacency matrices, where each entry indicates whether a pair of species interacts (1) or not (0). These matrices can be factorized using Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) [@Golub1971], a mathematical tool that provides complementary measures of network complexity. The first is the rank of the adjacency matrix, which reflects the number of linearly independent interaction patterns. Ecologically, this can be interpreted as the number of distinct interaction “strategies” present in the system. The second measure applies Shannon’s entropy [@Shannon1948] to the distribution of non-zero singular values obtained from the SVD. While this “SVD entropy” has been proposed as an indicator of internal informational balance [@Strydom2021], empirical comparisons with randomized networks show that random structures tend to maximize entropy, whereas ecological networks exhibit lower entropy due to their organized heterogeneity. For this reason, we interpret ecological complexity in terms of $1 -$ SVD entropy, which highlights the departure from randomness and captures the non-random organization characteristic of ecological systems. 

## Methods[f][g] 

### Study Areas

We analyzed seven marine trophic networks[h] distributed along a broad latitudinal gradient in the Southwest Atlantic, spanning from temperate to Antarctic ecosystems. The northernmost sites included San Jorge Gulf (~45–47°S, 65–68°W; [@Funes2022]) and the Beagle Channel (~54°S, 68°W; [@Rodriguez2022]), both representing sub-Antarctic coastal systems characterized by high productivity and seasonal variability. Further east, the Burdwood Bank (~54°S, 59°W; [@Marina2024a]) and the Scotia Sea (~48–58°S, 50°W; [@Lopez-Lopez2022]) encompass shelf and oceanic environments influenced by the confluence of the Antarctic Circumpolar Current and the Malvinas Current. In the Antarctic region, we included Potter Cove (62°S, 58°W; [@Marina2018]) and the Weddell Sea (74–78°S, 30°W; [@Jacob2011]), which represent coastal and deep continental shelf systems under polar conditions. Together, these sites capture a wide range of oceanographic, biogeographical, and trophic contexts across nearly 30 degrees of latitude in the Southern Hemisphere. A detailed description of each study site can be found in [@Marina2024b].



To ensure comparability across sites, we standardized some of the source networks following a common trophic classification scheme. In the Beagle Channel and Scotia Sea networks, we incorporated marine mammals and seabirds that were not included in the original publications. For the Weddell Sea, we simplified the basal compartment by collapsing planktonic microalgae that were included at the species level. These adjustments harmonized the trophic resolution among systems while preserving their key ecological structure, allowing robust cross-site comparisons along the latitudinal gradient.[i][j]

![](Figures/TIM_SouthernOceanStressorsSites.png)




### Human Impact Index[k][l][m]

To assess the impact of anthropogenic stressors, including climate change, we used the cumulative human impact index on the world's ocean, already used in network studies [@Mestre2022]. The cumulative human impact on the world's ocean considers the following impacts on global marine ecosystems: land-based stressors (nutrient pollution, organic and inorganic pollution, direct human light pollution); fishing-related stressors (demersal destructive, demersal non-destructive high and low bycatch, pelagic high and low bycatch, artisanal); climate change stressors (sea surface temperature anomalies, ultraviolet anomalies, ocean acidification) and ocean-based stressors (sea level rise, commercial shipping, invasive species, ocean-based pollution, benthic structures) [@Halpern2015]. We estimated the cumulative human impact index using the last version of the index [@Halpern2015] and calculated the mean over the area of each network.

### Network metrics[n][o]

We described the network structure and complexity with metrics that are widely used in food web studies [@Delmas2018; @Landi2018; @Montoya2006], such as link density, connectance,  mean trophic level, modularity, dynamic stability (maximal eigenvalue), and complexity (Entropy, rank Deficiency) (Table 1).

\tiny

| Network Metric         | Definition                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    | Ecological Meaning                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           | Reference                                              |
|------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
| Mean trophic level     | The trophic level (TL) of a species represents the number of links that separate it from the base of the food web and indicates the position the species occupies in the network.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     | Mean TL of a food web shows the efficiency of the energy transfer from basal assemblages to top predators and is related to stability. See Eq. (2) in Supplementary Material.                                                                                                              | @Borrelli2014; @Thompson2007   |
| Connectance            | Proportion of established interactions between species relative to all possible ones.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  | Estimator of food web robustness to perturbations and ecosystem stability. See Eq. (1) in Supplementary Material.                                                                                                                                                                           | @Delmas2018; @Dunne2002; @May1972 |
| Link density           | Mean number of trophic interactions (links) per species.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    | The average number of links per species informs about how connected species are within the food web.                                                                                                                                                                                         | @Dunne2002                                    |
| Modularity             | Modularity describes how densely sub-groups of species interact with one another compared to species from other sub-groups.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             | Modularity is positively associated with robustness, because perturbations can be retained within modules, preventing them from spreading to the whole network.                                                                                                                              | @May1972; @Stouffer2011              |
| Maximal Eigenvalue     | The maximal real part of the eigenvalues of the community matrix Jacobian for randomly parametrized systems, maintaining predator–prey structure. This indicates the tendency of the system to return to equilibrium after a perturbation.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     | A measure of dynamic stability: if the maximal real part is negative, the system returns to equilibrium after a perturbation. The distribution of this value across simulations provides insight into the network’s resilience to dynamic perturbations.                                       | @Allesina2008a                                   |
| SVD Complexity      | Entropy computed from the normalized singular values of the adjacency matrix obtained through Singular Value Decomposition (SVD). While entropy traditionally measures how evenly structural information is distributed across dimensions, randomized networks often show higher values than empirical food webs. We therefore use $ 1-$SVD entropy for complexity to emphasize deviations from randomness. | Ecological complexity arises from organized heterogeneity, not from randomness. Lower entropy (and thus higher $1 -$ entropy) indicates stronger structural constraints and non-random organization of interactions, consistent with ecological assembly processes. | @Shannon1948; @Loke2022; @Strydom2021; @Huaylla2024 |
| Rank Deficiency        | The relative rank deficiency is calculated as $D = 1 - (r / M)$, where $r$ is the adjacency matrix rank and $M$ is the maximum possible rank (number of species). It quantifies the number of rows/columns in the adjacency matrix that are linearly dependent.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 | Provides an estimate of external structural complexity: higher deficiency implies more redundancy in interactions (less unique strategies), while lower deficiency implies a more structurally unique and diverse network.                                                                  | @Strydom2021    |

Table 1: Network metrics used to describe the structure and complexity of food webs. The ecological meaning of each metric is also provided, along with references for further reading.

\normalsize

#### Dynamic stability

We used the maximal real part of the eigenvalues of the community matrix Jacobian [@Allesina2015a] for randomly parametrized systems, keeping fixed the predator–prey (sign) structure and assuming the system is stable [@Barabas2017]. This is a measure of the dynamic stability of the system, defined as its ability to return to equilibrium after a perturbation. The values of the entries of the Jacobian are sampled from a uniform distribution, assuming that the positive effect of preys on predators has a maximal value of 1 and the negative effect of predators on preys has a minimal value of -10 (representing a 10% ecological efficiency ). The diagonal entries of the Jacobian that represent the self-regulation of the species are sampled from a uniform distribution between *-SelfReg* and 0, with the value of *SelfReg* determined by simulations, given that 5% of the time the network is stable (That means that the maximal real part of the eigenvalues is negative). To find the value of *SelfReg*, we performed a grid search over the range of values from -7 to -60 in steps of 1, and for each value, we sampled 10000 Jacobians and calculated the proportion of stable systems. Then we interpolated the results to find the value of *SelfReg* that gives a proportion of stable systems of 0.05. Finally, for the value of *SelfReg* that we found, we performed 10000 simulations and selected only the stable ones to obtain a distribution of the maximal real part of the eigenvalues for each network. 

#### Estimating Network Complexity

Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) factorizes a matrix $\mathbf{A}$ into the form
$$
\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U} \, \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \, \mathbf{V}^T,
$$
where $\mathbf{U}$ and $\mathbf{V}$ are orthogonal matrices, the columns in these matrices are, respectively, the left- and right-singular vectors of $\mathbf{A}$. $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ is a diagonal matrix containing the non-negative singular values $\sigma_i$ of $\mathbf{A}$ in descending order. The singular values indicate the relative weight of each structural dimension represented in the matrix. 

When SVD is applied to a mean-centered data matrix or a covariance matrix, the squared singular values are equal to the eigenvalues of the covariance matrix and thus quantify the proportion of total variance explained by each principal component [@Jolliffe2002; @Golub2013]. In the context of ecological networks, the singular values measure the strength of each independent dimension's contribution to the overall network structure.

We used the normalized singular values to compute the SVD entropy, following @Shannon1948. The normalized singular values are:
$$
s_i = \frac{\sigma_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{k} \sigma_j},
$$
where $k$ is the number of non-zero singular values.

The SVD entropy $J$ is then defined as:
$$
J = - \frac{1}{\ln(k)} \sum_{i=1}^{k} s_i \ln(s_i),
$$
where the normalization by $\ln(k)$ ensures that the maximum entropy is 1, equivalent to Pielou's evenness index [@Pielou1975].

Shannon entropy, when applied to the normalized singular values of the adjacency matrix, quantifies how evenly structural information is distributed across independent dimensions of the network. Randomized networks with fixed connectance tend to have higher SVD entropy than empirical ecological networks (See Supplementary Material), because their structure is more homogeneous and information is spread evenly across many dimensions. In contrast, ecological networks typically display lower entropy, as a few dominant singular values capture most of the structure due to non-random ecological constraints such as trophic hierarchy, body-size scaling, or modularity.

This apparent paradox reflects a fundamental distinction: SVD entropy quantifies statistical uniformity (maximal disorder), whereas ecological complexity arises from organized heterogeneity. Random networks, therefore, tend to display higher entropy, but ecological networks exhibit lower-entropy structures that are more functionally meaningful. To better capture this, we define

$$
E = 1 - J
$$

and refer to it as SVD Complexity. 

In addition, we also estimated the complexity of ecological networks using the rank deficiency of the adjacency matrix. The rank deficiency is a measure of how many rows/columns in a matrix are not linearly independent, and therefore how many rows/columns can be expressed as linear combinations of others. In ecological terms, this means that some species or interactions can be described as a combination of others, which reduces the complexity of the network.

The rank of $A$ (noted as $k$) is the dimension of the vector space spanned by the matrix and corresponds to the number of linearly independent rows or columns; therefore, the maximum rank of a matrix $M$ will always be equal to the dimension of A, in the case of trophic networks A is square,  which ecologically speaking is the richness. A matrix is “full-ranked” when $k = M$, i.e., all of its rows/columns are unique. Matrices that are not full-ranked are called rank deficient, and we can measure rank deficiency using the relative rank deficiency to control for the difference in the number of trophic species of the different networks, expressing it as:

$D  = 1 - (r/M )$

where D = 0 means that the matrix is full-ranked, and D = 1 implies that the matrix is rank deficient. This serves as a coarse estimate of complexity, as the more unique columns/rows are in the matrix, the larger this value will be.

### Estimating variability of metrics using a metaweb model

We considered that the compiled and standardized food webs represent all possible interactions. If a species is not present at a local site at any given time, the set of interactions involving the species will not be realized; therefore, the actual number of species and interactions may fluctuate over time. To represent this variability, we employed a metaweb assembly model [@Saravia2022], assuming that each compiled food web represents the metaweb for its respective area.

In this model, species migrate from the metaweb to a local site with a probability $c$, and become extinct from the local site with probability $e$; a reminiscence of the theory of island biogeography [@MacArthur1967], but with the addition of the food web structure. Species migrate with their potential trophic interactions from the metaweb; then in the local site, only the interactions with the present species are realized. Species have a probability of secondary extinction $se$ if none of its prey are present, which only applies to non-basal species. When a species goes extinct locally, it may trigger secondary extinctions, which are modulated by $se$.

The model was implemented in R using the package `meweasmo` [@Saravia2022b]. These three parameters were fitted for each compiled food web using approximate Bayesian computation (ABC) [@Beaumont2010]. The ABC algorithm is implemented in the package `abc` [@Beaumont2010] using the `abc` function with the `rejection` method.[p] The distance function used was the Euclidean distance between the observed and simulated number of species and connectance. The prior distributions for the parameters were considered uniform: $c \sim U(0.01,0.3)$, $e \sim U(0.001,0.5)$, and $se \sim U(0.1,0.9)$. The number of simulations was set to 10000 and the tolerance to 0.05.

#### Statistical analyses

We modelled the relationships between multiple network metrics and environmental covariates using a multilevel multivariate Bayesian regression framework [@McElreath2020]. Seven response metrics were modelled jointly: connectance (C), SVD complexity, modularity, mean trophic level, link density, rank deficiency, and a dynamic stability metric; allowing residual covariation among metrics to be estimated and uncertainty to be propagated across responses.

Because several metrics are bounded or skewed, we applied scale‐preserving transformations prior to modelling. Proportion metrics (C, Modularity, Rank Deficiency) were logit‐transformed, strictly positive variables (Link Density and dynamic stability) were log‐transformed, and SVD complexity and mean trophic level were standardized (z‐scores). Dynamic stability, quantified as the negative maximum eigenvalue of the community matrix, is strictly negative; therefore, values were multiplied by −1 and modelled as log(−maximum eigenvalue) to place them on a positive, approximately log‐normal scale.

Each response metric was regressed on the same set of predictors: number of trophic species (S$$), log‐transformed area (km^2^), latitude, mean human impact index, and depth. We included $S$ as a covariate, since variation in the number of nodes can strongly influence network metrics. Here, $S$ represents the set of trophic groups documented in each food web, not a complete census of biological species in the region, as the networks were assembled to capture trophic interactions rather than to provide exhaustive species inventories. Including $S$ allowed us to account for its potential influence and to identify broader patterns in network structure that are not solely driven by species richness.

All predictors were centered and scaled prior to analysis. A site‐level varying intercept was included to account for within‐site dependence arising from multiple metaweb‐derived replicates per local network, thereby avoiding pseudo‐replication. Site‐level intercepts were estimated separately for each response, allowing networks to differ in their mean metric values. Residuals across the seven responses were modelled using a full residual correlation matrix, enabling inference on co‐variation among network properties after accounting for fixed effects and site‐level shifts.

Weakly informative priors were used for all regression coefficients. Because fitting a fully joint multivariate model is computationally demanding, analyses were conducted using a subset of 200 simulated replicates per site, allowing full multivariate estimation within feasible computation time. Model adequacy was evaluated using standard diagnostics, including inspection of chain convergence (R-hat and effective sample size), trace plots, and posterior predictive checks tailored to each response metric by comparing observed and replicated distributions on the original scales.

Partial dependence (marginal) effect curves for each predictor–response combination were obtained from posterior linear predictor draws, conditioning on other covariates at their means and excluding group‐level effects. These effects were back‐transformed to the original metric scales for visualization.

All analyses were conducted in R version 4.3.1 [@RCoreTeam2023] using the `brms` package [@Burkner2017] with the `CmdStan` backend [@StanDevelopmentTeam2025]. All data transformations, model specifications, priors, and code required to reproduce the analyses are provided in the Supplementary Materials and in a public GitHub repository (https://github.com/EcoComplex/SouthMetawebs), with archived code and data available on Zenodo (doi: xxxxxxxx).



## Results[q][r]

\begin{table}[ht]
\centering[s]
\begin{tabular}{lcccccc}
  \toprule
Network & C & LD & TL & SVD Complexity & Rank Deficiency & Modularity \\ 
  \midrule
Burdwood Bank & \shortstack{0.014 \\ {\tiny(0.012--0.018)}} & \shortstack{4.463 \\ {\tiny(3.937--4.810)}} & \shortstack{2.470 \\ {\tiny(2.377--2.551)}} & \shortstack{0.079 \\ {\tiny(0.075--0.082)}} & \shortstack{0.635 \\ {\tiny(0.572--0.660)}} & \shortstack{0.334 \\ {\tiny(0.301--0.371)}} \\ 
Gulf San Jorge & \shortstack{0.038 \\ {\tiny(0.035--0.042)}} & \shortstack{5.590 \\ {\tiny(4.750--6.121)}} & \shortstack{2.906 \\ {\tiny(2.653--3.025)}} & \shortstack{0.080 \\ {\tiny(0.075--0.084)}} & \shortstack{0.447 \\ {\tiny(0.419--0.481)}} & \shortstack{0.137 \\ {\tiny(0.074--0.212)}} \\ 
Potter Cove & \shortstack{0.055 \\ {\tiny(0.050--0.060)}} & \shortstack{5.461 \\ {\tiny(4.639--5.935)}} & \shortstack{2.233 \\ {\tiny(2.094--2.530)}} & \shortstack{0.091 \\ {\tiny(0.085--0.096)}} & \shortstack{0.493 \\ {\tiny(0.451--0.533)}} & \shortstack{0.164 \\ {\tiny(0.054--0.243)}} \\ 
Beagle Channel & \shortstack{0.048 \\ {\tiny(0.045--0.052)}} & \shortstack{7.256 \\ {\tiny(6.433--7.855)}} & \shortstack{2.607 \\ {\tiny(2.480--2.798)}} & \shortstack{0.092 \\ {\tiny(0.088--0.096)}} & \shortstack{0.411 \\ {\tiny(0.386--0.439)}} & \shortstack{0.119 \\ {\tiny(0.058--0.180)}} \\ 
Northern Scotia & \shortstack{0.165 \\ {\tiny(0.157--0.174)}} & \shortstack{38.458 \\ {\tiny(34.339--41.574)}} & \shortstack{3.549 \\ {\tiny(3.431--3.683)}} & \shortstack{0.172 \\ {\tiny(0.167--0.175)}} & \shortstack{0.455 \\ {\tiny(0.434--0.476)}} & \shortstack{0.076 \\ {\tiny(0.001--0.113)}} \\ 
Southern Scotia & \shortstack{0.151 \\ {\tiny(0.143--0.158)}} & \shortstack{31.339 \\ {\tiny(27.805--33.969)}} & \shortstack{3.513 \\ {\tiny(3.374--3.649)}} & \shortstack{0.167 \\ {\tiny(0.162--0.171)}} & \shortstack{0.424 \\ {\tiny(0.400--0.447)}} & \shortstack{0.071 \\ {\tiny(0.049--0.120)}} \\ 
Weddell Sea & \shortstack{0.063 \\ {\tiny(0.059--0.075)}} & \shortstack{24.084 \\ {\tiny(21.360--26.175)}} & \shortstack{3.443 \\ {\tiny(3.170--3.757)}} & \shortstack{0.155 \\ {\tiny(0.148--0.159)}} & \shortstack{0.707 \\ {\tiny(0.669--0.724)}} & \shortstack{0.219 \\ {\tiny(0.171--0.245)}} \\ 
   \bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Summary of network metrics across 1000 simulations for each metaweb. Values are means with 95\% confidence intervals in parentheses. 
Metrics: C = connectance, LD = link density, TL = mean trophic level, SVD Complexity = singular value decomposition–based complexity, Rank Deficiency = proportion of redundant trophic roles, and Modularity = strength of network compartmentalization.} 
\label{tab:metaweb_metrics}
\end{table}

Across the seven food web analyzed, network metrics varied markedly in magnitude and variability (Table 2). Connectance (C) and link density (LD) spanned an order of magnitude, with the highest values observed in the Scotia Arc networks and the lowest in the Burdwood Bank. Mean trophic level (TL) ranged between 2.2 and 3.5, whereas SVD complexity—-a measure of network structural organization-—showed moderate variation among systems. Rank deficiency, reflecting the effective dimensionality of interaction matrices, was generally lower in the northern systems, while modularity displayed a wide range across networks, indicating differing degrees of compartmentalization.

Generalized additive mixed models (GAMMs) revealed clear spatial patterns in the structural properties of marine food webs along the Southwest Atlantic (Figure 1). S[t][u][v]VD complexity increased significantly toward higher latitudes (p = 0.032; Table S1), indicating that high-latitude food webs are more internally organized and hierarchically structured, consistent with greater network complexity in colder environments. Latitude explained 13.1 % of the total deviance, underscoring the strong latitudinal gradient in network organization. Local dynamical stability, estimated from the maximum eigenvalue, also varied significantly with latitude (p = 0.045;[w][x] Figure 1, middle panel), showing a weak but consistent nonlinear pattern. This pattern suggests that more negative eigenvalues at higher latitudes indicate enhanced resilience to perturbations in polar systems. This was the only relationship exhibiting a detectable degree of nonlinearity (Table S1). However, the model explained only 19.1 % of the deviance, indicating greater variability in stability across sites. Finally, connectance (C) was positively related to depth (p = 0.0019; Figure 1, right panel), revealing denser interaction networks in deeper or more pelagic ecosystems, such as those of the Scotia Arc. Depth explained  28.4 % of the total deviance, highlighting a strong spatial signal in the distribution of interaction density along depth gradients.

![Relationships between food-web structural and stability metrics and environmental gradients across seven Southern Ocean and sub-Antarctic marine ecosystems. Panels show fitted Generalized Additive Mixed Model (GAMM) smooths for (left) SVD complexity versus latitude, (center) dynamical stability (maximum eigenvalue) versus latitude, and (right) connectance ($C$) versus depth. Colored points represent empirical networks, with vertical bars indicating 95% intervals from 500 metaweb-based replicates. SVD complexity increased significantly with latitude, indicating greater internal organization toward higher latitudes. Stability, represented by the maximum eigenvalue, also increased in magnitude (more negative) with latitude, suggesting higher stability in polar food webs. Connectance increased with depth, reflecting denser interaction structure in pelagic networks.](Figures/metric_SVDC_MAXE_C_vs_lat_area_ci_gam.png)

Modularity was significantly related to the number of trophic species (S) (p = 0.0002), area (p = 0.0188), and latitude (p = 0.0340; Figure 2). Modularity increased with S, suggesting that food webs with more trophic species exhibit stronger internal organization. In contrast, modularity declined with increasing area,[y][z] indicating that larger systems are less compartmentalized, while its weak negative association with latitude suggests a tendency toward lower modularity at higher latitudes.[aa][ab][ac][ad] Together, these effects explained 74.3% of the total deviance, demonstrating that both local complexity and spatial context influence food-web compartmentalization.

![Relationships between food-web modularity and ecological predictors across seven Southern Ocean and sub-Antarctic marine ecosystems. Each panel shows the fitted Generalized Additive Mixed Model (GAMM) smooth for modularity as a function of (left) the number of trophic species ($S$), (center) log-transformed ecosystem area, and (right) latitude. Colored points represent empirical networks, and vertical bars indicate 95% intervals from 500 metaweb-based replicates. Modularity increased significantly with the number of trophic species, but decreased with both ecosystem area and latitude, indicating simpler modular structures in larger and more northerly ecosystems.](Figures/metric_Mod_vs_S_lat_area_sig_gam.png)

Mean trophic level (TL) increased significantly with log-transformed area (p = 0.0085), indicating that larger systems tended to support longer trophic chains (Figure 3).[ae]

Rank deficiency, a measure of structural redundancy, increased significantly with the number of trophic species (p < 0.0001), suggesting that larger networks contain more redundant interactions. Conversely, it decreased with human impact (p = 0.0023), implying that anthropogenic disturbance erodes structural redundancy within marine food webs. The variables explained 14 % and 3.9% of the total deviance respectively.

![Relationships between food-web structural properties and environmental or network predictors across seven Southern Ocean and sub-Antarctic marine ecosystems. Panels show fitted Generalized Additive Mixed Model (GAMM) smooths for (left) mean trophic level (TL) versus log-transformed area, (center) rank deficiency versus human impact, and (right) rank deficiency versus number of trophic species ($S$). Colored points represent empirical networks, with vertical bars indicating 95% confidence intervals from 500 metaweb-based replicates. Mean trophic level increased significantly with ecosystem area, suggesting deeper or more extensive systems host longer food chains. Rank deficiency decreased with increasing human impact, indicating structural simplification under disturbance, and increased with $S$, consistent with higher dimensionality in more species-rich networks](Figures/metric_TL_RD_vs_lat_area_ci_gam.png)

## Discussion

At higher latitudes, food-web networks tend to display greater complexity and stability, which could be linked to the strong seasonality characteristic of these ecosystems. In particular, high-latitude food webs, by incorporating all potential interactions, also reflect the structural reorganization that occurs during winter, when extreme environmental conditions alter resource availability and trophic activity of species [@Humphries2017]. There are no studies that directly link stability with latitude in marine food webs, here we observed greater complexity, which could be linked to greater stability at higher latitudes. But there is still a great discussion about the link between complexity and stability in food webs [@May1972; @Allesina2015; @Jacquet2016], perhaps our results are related to the use of 1 - SVD Entropy, as a measure of complexity that captures the non-random organization of interactions in ecological networks.



Given the widely accepted view that modularity can enhance food‐web stability by limiting the propagation of perturbations [@Eskuche-Keith2023], we initially expected a positive relationship between modularity and the human impact index. However, no such relationship was detected. One possible explanation is that human pressures may simultaneously promote and erode modularity through opposing mechanisms. While disturbance can favor compartmentalization by isolating impacts, chronic anthropogenic stressors often homogenize ecosystems by selectively removing sensitive species and interactions, thereby reducing network compartmentalization. This interpretation is consistent with the findings of @Mestre2022, who showed that fishing pressure can erode modular structure in marine food webs. The combined action of these opposing processes could result in a net neutral relationship between modularity and overall human impact in our analysis. Moreover, the composite nature of the human impact index may mask contrasting effects of its individual components on modularity and other network metrics. Disentangling these component‐specific effects represents an important avenue for future research.

## References



[a]No estoy segura pero esta parte no iría en métodos?
[b]a mi tambien me parecio un cambio de ritmo en el texto importante, queda de intro de paper metodologico
[c]Sí, esto era como una intro a esos métodos y quedó suelto acá, para ver si lo dejábamos o lo sacábamos.
[d]Coincido
[e]Esto es una propuesta tuya no, Leo? capaz se puede poner algo para enfatizar que es novedoso. Algo como: "We propose using the transformed metric $1- $ SVD as a measure of the organized heterogeneity of ecological systems"
[f]Sugiero la siguiente organización para esta sección
### Study region
### Food web data, standardization and variability representation
### Food web analysis
[g]Para no hacer mucho lío, cree una nueva sección Methods en un documento aparte siguiendo el formato. https://docs.google.com/document/d/1FFwwB3wauffy7rlw3_pEpmzfM65ODbMyLInhrtdYLNs/edit?usp=sharing
[h]Soy partidario de usar el término más popular: food webs.
[i]Este párrafo lo podría en sección de "Network metrics" .
[j]Coincido
[k]Creo que va a ser más claro para lectores que la expliación del índice vaya junto con las otras variables usadas como indepndientes en el análisis, podría ser una sección aparte antes de  Statistical analyses, o sino en esa misma sección como está ahora que al comienzo se describen qué variables se usan en el análisis
[l]En mi opinión, ya charlada con Leo, hay que quitar este índice del manuscrito porque: 1) le quita protagonismo al objeto de análisis del paper que, a mi entender, es la variación de la complejidad, estructura y estabilidad de las redes tróficas en un gradiente latitudinal; 2) está desactualizado (es del 2015 según el artículo de Mestre et al. 2022) y la data que utiliza el índice para el Atlántico Sudoccidental es de baja calidad; y 3) merece atención en un artículo en particular donde realmente evaluemos el impacto de los estresores en las redes tróficas.
[m]Se publicó este paper con la actualización: 

Cumulative impacts to global marine ecosystems projected to more than double by mid-century
Benjamin S. Halpern 
DOI: 10.1126/science.adv2906
[n]Unificaría en una única sección todo lo referente a las métricas, en esta versión hay parte acá y luego separado Dynamic stability y las 2 de complexity
[o]Coincido
[p]Estos detalles de qué paquete y función se usaron sin ninguna explicación metodológica los pondría al final.
[q]En las figuras las relaciones que me parecen evidentes gráficamente son en Fig 1 SVD con Depth, en Fig 3 TL con log Area y con S, (y con Human Index pero como regresión x quantil, no llineal).  Para confirmar, pero creo Leo que ya me confirmaste: las figuras están echas descontando el efecto de todos los otros predictores (= partial regression plot)?
[r]Si, son efectos marginales.
[s]Mirando la tabla me desorientó el orden de los sitios, que no es alfabético, me parece que presentar los sitios de norte a sur ayudaría a la interpretación
[t]me cuesta mucho seguir los colores, poner tambien formas al punto central? redondo, triangulo y cuadrado, mas los colores? el mayor problema es que los tonos cambian si estan o no en la banda CI
[u]Si, voy a reacer esta figura
[v]Coincido
[w]esto es el 4% de la deviansa, y por la regla que se puso en el final de M&M respecto a interpretar los p, creo que capaz deberia leerse como no significativo
[x]Cierto, antes que no tenia agregado el factor aleatorio por sitio, daba todo significativo, entonces había que ser muy selectivo, ahora que ya no da todo significativo habría que pensar de mantener ese criterio o no. Si se mantuviera quedarían RankDeficiency - S, TLmean - log_area, Modularity - S, C  - depth_m como significativos.
[y]Lo que comentamos en la reunión: si el área aumenta, la heterogeneidad del habitat debería ser mayor llevando a una mayor modularidad. Si este resultado se debe al tipo de modularidad que se está calculando aquí, deberíamos ponerlo en métodos o por escrito en alguna parte
[z]En realidad da igual si lo calculamos con otro algoritmo de modularidad, como cluster_spinglass
[aa]quizá aquí añadir algo como que vemos esa tendencia porque Burdwook Bank sale muy alto? Outlier?
[ab]Si, es cierto Burdwood y Weddell son como un grupo aparte
[ac]no creo sea significativa, el p es 0.034 (cerca de 0.05) explica menos del 1% y un solo valor de los ajustados cae en la banda del 95%...
[ad]Si, estoy de acuerdo.
[ae]yo veo este plot y todas las medias observadas cae en la banda predicha, el p es pequeño 0.008... pero explica menos del 0.5%... es como que el factor sitio se apropia de la variabilidad explicada por el area. no?
